question.wording: What was the primary design goal of XML?

choices.choice: To replace HTML on the web
To carry and store data in a readable way
To execute client-side scripts
To format text for printed documents

question.wording: Which syntax rule is mandatory for all XML documents?

choices.choice: Having at least two root elements
Mandatory use of the Latin alphabet
All tags must be properly closed
Not allowing attributes in tags

question.wording: How is an optional element (zero or one time) defined in a DTD?

choices.choice: Using the + symbol
Using the * symbol
Using the ? symbol
No symbol is used

question.wording: What is the difference between a "Well-Formed" and a "Valid" document?

choices.choice: They are identical concepts in the XML specification
Valid complies with a DTD; Well-Formed only with syntax
Valid cannot contain attributes
Well-Formed requires an XSLT stylesheet

question.wording: What is an XML Namespace?

choices.choice: A directory where .xml files are stored
A mechanism to avoid element name conflicts
A reserved tag for the root element
The name of the server hosting the document

question.wording: Which entity type is used to represent the less-than character (<) in XML?

choices.choice: &
<
>
"

question.wording: What does the reserved word EMPTY mean in a DTD element declaration?

choices.choice: The element can only contain white spaces
The element cannot have content (children or text)
The element is optional for the user
The element deletes previous data

question.wording: What is the purpose of the prologue in an XML document?

choices.choice: To indicate the version and character encoding
To define the visual style of the document
To list the names of the file authors
To close the root element at the end of the file

question.wording: What does the #REQUIRED value mean in an attribute declaration (ATTLIST)?

choices.choice: The attribute has a fixed default value
The attribute is optional
The author must include the attribute in the element
The attribute only accepts integers

question.wording: What is the hierarchical structure of an XML document?

choices.choice: Network structure
Tree structure
Linear or sequential structure
Relational structure

question.wording: Which data type in a DTD allows only raw letters and numbers?

choices.choice: #PCDATA
CDATA
IDREF
ENTITY

question.wording: What is the limit of root elements in an XML file?

choices.choice: There is no limit
Minimum two
Exactly one
One for each DTD schema

question.wording: What is a processing instruction (<? ... ?>) used for?

choices.choice: To comment out lines of code
To pass information to the application reading the XML
To define new user tags
To validate the document against a DTD

question.wording: How is a list of possible values for an attribute defined in a DTD?

choices.choice: Using the #LIST symbol
Listing values inside parentheses separated by |
Defining a child element for each value
It is not possible to limit values in a DTD

question.wording: What is an XML Parser?

choices.choice: A program that translates XML to binary
Software that reads the document and verifies its structure
A computer virus that deletes tags
A text editor specialized in code

question.wording: Which symbol indicates that an element can appear unlimited times (0 or more)?
Can an XML tag name start with a number?

choices.choice: Yes, if the number is followed by a letter
No, never
Only if defined that way in the DTD
Only in version 1.1 documents

question.wording: What does the term "Normalized Attribute" mean?

choices.choice: An attribute that has been translated into English
An attribute where white spaces have been processed
An attribute that has no value
An attribute that repeats in all elements

question.wording: What is the reserved attribute 'xml:lang' used for?

choices.choice: To automatically translate the document
To identify the language of the element's content
To change the server-side programming language
It's an error, that attribute doesn't exist

question.wording: What is the main limitation of a DTD compared to an XML Schema (XSD)?

choices.choice: DTDs are harder to write
DTDs do not support complex data types or numeric ranges
DTDs are not compatible with modern browsers
DTDs only allow 10 elements per file
